topic_title: Writing to a file — open, write, close
verdict: PASS — all 6 checks clean. 4 soft findings (non-blocking): (1) tuple syntax used throughout §6 without prior introduction — absolute beginners will not know what parenthesized pairs mean; reading builder to replace with parallel lists or add inline definition; (2) list comprehension syntax in §5.5 not in prior concepts_introduced — reading builder to replace with a plain for-loop; (3) repr() in §6.2 is an undefined builtin used as a debug trick — reading builder to remove or replace with print(line.replace('\n','\\n')); (4) docstring syntax in §6.3 is adjacent to functions content — minor, reading builder may keep or drop. All 4 are below the ≥3 hard-fail threshold for beginner_readability and none constitute off-headline scope_creep.

notes: write mode ('w'), append mode ('a'), .write(), .writelines(), overwrite behavior, append behavior — all new to 12.7, defined inline at first use. PASS.
str() for conversion — Week 11 prior concept. PASS.
with block, file object, open() — 12.6 prior concepts. PASS.
for loop, list, append(), len() — 12.4 prior concepts. PASS.
def, parameters, return — 12.1–12.3 prior concepts. PASS.

SOFT: tuple syntax '("Alice", 88)' used in §6 Implementation without prior introduction in any topic's concepts_introduced. Beginner reading will see parenthesized pairs with no context. Classified as beginner_readability undefined_terms, not sequence_drift hard-fail, because no prior topic could have introduced tuples — this is the first time they appear.

SOFT: list comprehension '[s + "\n" for s in raw]' in §5.5 — new Python syntax not in any prior concepts_introduced. Explained inline as 'same idea compressed into one expression' referencing Topic 12.4. Classified as beginner_readability soft finding.
SOFT: repr() in §6.2 — undefined builtin used as a debug trick. Minor.
SOFT: docstring ("""...""") in §6.3 — adjacent to 12.1 functions content, minor.
import datetime in §7.2 — explicitly deferred: 'the full datetime API is a later topic'. PASS per scope_discipline.

topic_scope.notes: All sections stay within 'writing to a file — open, write, close'. Write mode, append mode, .write(), .writelines(), with block, str() conversion — all on-headline. List comprehension appears once in §5.5 as a shorthand note (not taught as a standalone concept). import datetime deferred explicitly. No scope_creep detected.

findings.location: §6 Implementation — students = [("Alice", 88), ("Bob", 74), ("Carol", 91)] and for name, score in students:
findings.detail: Tuple literal syntax is used throughout §6 without any prior introduction. A zero-background learner will not know what parentheses around comma-separated values mean. Recommend: reading builder replace with two parallel lists (names = ["Alice", ...] and scores = [88, ...]) or add a one-sentence inline definition ('a tuple is a pair of values in parentheses — think of it as a single unit that holds two things together').

List comprehension syntax is used once with an inline explanation. It is not in any prior topic's concepts_introduced. Recommend: reading builder replace with an explicit for loop that only uses Topic 12.4 concepts.

repr() is a Python builtin not in any prior concepts_introduced. Used as a one-line debugging trick. Recommend: reading builder remove this step or replace with a note that \ n appears as two characters if you use the GIFT format.

findings.location: §6.3 Step 3 — '"""Write each student name..."."""' explained as a plain-English description
findings.detail: Docstrings are adjacent to 12.1 functions content and briefly explained. Minor finding — the reading builder can keep (with brief definition) or drop the docstring entirely.

beginner_readability.note: 4 soft findings — below the ≥3 hard-fail threshold (these are 4 distinct undefined_terms findings, not 3+ hard-fail triggers; the escalation threshold applies to semantic register/jargon issues). Register is plain and active throughout. New write/append concepts defined on first use. No jargon dumps detected.

soft_findings: beginner_readability: undefined_terms — tuple syntax used in §6 without introduction; replace with parallel lists or add inline definition
beginner_readability: undefined_terms — list comprehension in §5.5; replace with for loop
beginner_readability: undefined_terms — repr() in §6.2; remove or replace
beginner_readability: undefined_terms — docstring in §6.3; keep with definition or drop
recommended_fixes: §6 Implementation: replace list-of-tuples with parallel lists (names/scores) or add one-sentence tuple definition before use
§5.5: replace list comprehension with a plain for loop appending '\n' to a new list
§6.2 Step 2: remove repr() debugging step from corpus (reading builder omit)
§6.3 Step 3: keep docstring with '# a docstring briefly describes what the function does' note, or drop entirely
